claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 7 years old and does not live with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 7 (under 16)
  - Claimant contributes £50.00/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
  - Child in local authority care for 12 weeks (>8) and does not spend 24+ hours/week at home
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Child in local authority care for 12 weeks (>8) and does not spend 24+ hours/week at home
circumstances: Claimant contributes £50.00/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
Child in local authority care for 12 weeks (>8) and does not spend 24+ hours/week at home
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you a few questions.

Let's start with: **Do you have any children you're responsible for, and if so, how many?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I have one child - my son Alex. He's 7 years old. I'd like to know if I can claim Child Benefit for him.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing that information! I can see you have a 7-year-old son named Alex.

Now, to assess your eligibility, I need to ask: **Do you live in the UK?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great! And the next question is: **Does Alex live with you?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he doesn't live with me.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I understand. Since Alex doesn't live with you, I need to ask: **Do you make financial contributions towards Alex's upkeep (such as for food, clothes, pocket money, or accommodation), and if so, how much do you contribute?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I do contribute towards his upkeep. I help with things like clothes, school expenses, and other things he needs. I give about £50 a week.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. That's helpful to know. 

One more important question: **Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex (for example, the person he lives with)?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for him.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Good news! You are eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.**

Here's why:

- Alex is 7 years old, which is under the age of 16, so he meets the age requirement
- Although Alex doesn't live with you, you're making financial contributions of £50 per week towards his upkeep (clothes, school expenses, etc.)
- To qualify for Child Benefit when a child doesn't live with you, you need to contribute at least the same amount as the Child Benefit payment itself (currently £25.60 per week for an eldest/only child)
- Your contribution of £50 per week exceeds this threshold
- Most importantly, nobody else is currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex

Since you meet all these conditions, you qualify as being "responsible" for Alex under the Child Benefit rules, and you can claim Child Benefit for him.

**Important note:** If you or your partner have an individual income over £60,000, you may need to pay a High Income Child Benefit tax charge. However, you can still claim the benefit.

You can proceed with making a claim for Child Benefit for Alex. Is there anything else you'd like to know about the process?

reasonings: Alex is 7 years old, which is under 16, so he meets the age requirement. Although Alex does not live with the claimant, the claimant is making financial contributions of £50 per week towards Alex's upkeep (including clothes and school expenses). According to the rules, you can qualify for Child Benefit if you're paying at least the same amount as Child Benefit towards looking after the child. The current Child Benefit rate is £25.60 per week for the eldest/only child, and the claimant's contribution of £50 per week exceeds this amount. Additionally, nobody else is currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex. Therefore, the claimant qualifies for Child Benefit for Alex.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant has one child, Alex (age 7), who does not live with them. However, the claimant contributes £50 per week towards Alex's upkeep, which is well above the Child Benefit amount. Since nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex, and the claimant meets the responsibility test through their financial contributions, they are eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex.

The current Child Benefit rate is £25.60 per week for the eldest/only child, and the claimant's contribution of £50 per week exceeds this amount. Additionally, nobody else is currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex. Therefore, the claimant qualifies for Child Benefit for Alex.

Since nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex, and the claimant meets the responsibility test through their financial contributions, they are eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex.
